item.text: Baseline is clean: levels 1–3 solve in 103 moves, replay-valid. I’m now narrowing inspection to the level dispatch, the existing leg interfaces, and perception’s compact observation helpers before probing a level-4 clone.

item.command: /bin/zsh -lc "sed -n '1,24p' solve.py; sed -n '1,24p' players.py; sed -n '1,210p' perception.py; sed -n '1,220p' gkm_try.py"

item.text: The first experiment identifies a selected movable object: arrows translate it on a 3-pixel lattice, while `USE` changes selection (the unique black center moves from `(36,54)` to `(21,24)`). Level 4 therefore looks like another multi-object placement puzzle, but with a different target relation; I’m cycling selections on clones and extracting each shape’s offsets and marker-center colors before choosing whether an existing leg fits.

The target relation is now geometrically verified on clones: the movable `6` plus can cover all three `12`-center rings at center `(30,15)`, and the movable `10` X can cover all three `14`-center rings at `(30,39)`. This gives a concrete dense measure—covered ring centers, from 0 to 6—and shows that the older single-color cover leg is close but not directly applicable. I’m adding a small generalized colored-group cover leg, then will validate the placements and real reward.

item.aggregated_output: RESULT levels=3 moves=103 replay_ok=True err=ValueError: no one-to-one shape/ring-group cover found

item.text: The first implementation attempt made no committed level-4 moves—the leg rejected its own inferred assignment before acting. I’m tightening the observational shape extraction now; the likely issue is restoration semantics when a moved pixel leaves a non-background marker, not the geometric hypothesis itself.

item.command: /bin/zsh -lc 'python -m py_compile legs.py players.py solve.py perception.py probe_l4.py && MPLCONFIGDIR=/tmp/gkm-mpl python gkm_try.py'
/bin/zsh -lc 'python -m py_compile legs.py players.py solve.py perception.py probe_l4.py && MPLCONFIGDIR=/tmp/gkm-mpl python gkm_try.py'

item.text: Both inferred placements executed exactly (24 level-4 actions), but the sparse reward did not fire. That falsifies “cover all six centers” as the complete win condition. I’m comparing the placed frame against the start and testing the remaining interaction context on a clone; likely the colored palette encodes an additional overlap/result constraint rather than simple coverage.

The post-placement frame confirms all six ring centers are physically covered, yet no reward—so coverage is only a dense proxy, not the actual condition. The marker geometry leaves just one full-cover pair, which we tested. I’m now doing a bounded symbolic search over only marker-related candidate placements (not raw frame BFS) to identify the missing relational constraint from reward.
